content.text: Creating Context and Providing a Value
React Context provides a way to pass data through the component tree without threading props through every intermediate layer. The mechanism involves three steps: creating a context object with
, wrapping a subtree with a Provider component that supplies the value, and reading the value in any descendant with
. This sub-subtopic focuses on the first two steps — creating and providing.

attrs.alt: Context tree: Provider at top, consumers access value directly

content.text: is called at the module level — outside any component. It returns a context object with two properties: a
component (the Consumer is the legacy API;

when there is no matching Provider above the component in the tree. It is not the initial value of the Provider; it is a fallback for consumers rendered outside any Provider. A well-chosen default (or explicit
The default value is only used when a consumer is rendered with no matching Provider in its ancestor tree. It does not affect consumers that are inside a Provider — those always get the Provider's
component wraps any part of the tree that needs access to the value. Every consumer that calls
with this context object will receive the current value from the nearest Provider above it in the tree.
prop is what consumers receive. When this prop changes (by reference), React re-renders all consumers of this context — even if they are not otherwise in the re-render path of the Provider's parent.
In practice, the Provider's value is almost always derived from component state. The pattern is to create a wrapper component that owns the state and passes it down via context.
owns the state and provides both the current value and the setter. Consumers can read
without needing the Provider as a prop.

serves two purposes: it satisfies TypeScript's type inference, and it provides a value when no Provider is present. Both matter in testing, where components may be rendered without a Provider wrapper.
createContext Must Be Called at Module Level

must be called at the module level, not inside a component or function. Calling it inside a component would create a new context object on every render, breaking the reference identity that React uses to match consumers to providers.
A single context type can have multiple Providers at different levels of the tree. Each consumer uses the value from the nearest Provider above it. This allows nested overrides.
The standard pattern is to export the context alongside a custom hook that wraps
and adds an error check for missing Providers. This way, consuming code gets a useful error message instead of a silent
The context itself is not exported — only the custom hook is. This enforces that consumers always go through
, which provides the error check. Direct
calls in consumer components are avoided.

When the Provider's value is an object created inline, it is a new reference on every render of the Provider's parent. This causes all consumers to re-render, even if the object's contents are unchanged. Memoising the value with
actually changes, not on every unrelated re-render of the parent component that wraps the Provider.
is stable across renders — React guarantees it never changes. So it does not need to be in the
dependency array, but including it is harmless.
Context vs Props: When to Choose Context
Component is tied to this app's structure

Real applications typically have several independent contexts: one for authentication, one for theming, one for UI state, one for locale. Each is its own context object and Provider. Consumers read only the contexts they actually need.
Consumers subscribe to each context independently. A component that reads only the theme will not re-render when the authenticated user changes — the two are separate subscriptions.
When there are multiple Providers, they nest, and the tree can become deeply indented. A common solution is a single
component that composes all Providers in one place.
The order of nesting matters only when Providers read from each other. If
on the outside. Otherwise, the order is a stylistic preference.
React DevTools shows context objects by a generated name unless you set
on the context object. This makes it much easier to identify which contexts are in use when debugging.
A consistent file organisation makes context code easy to find and maintain. The most common pattern is one file per context:
Understanding what triggers consumer re-renders prevents performance surprises. Consumers re-render when the Provider's
prop changes by reference. The Provider's parent re-rendering does not trigger consumer re-renders unless the value reference also changes.
The Provider's parent re-renders (new object each time)
Every render — always a new reference
Context works in server-rendered React (Next.js, Remix) with one constraint: the default value of
and any initial state must be serialisable if the Provider's value is injected from the server. State-based Providers (like
) initialise on the client; server-side passes initial values via props.
Setting up context requires two steps:
at module level to create the context object, and a
component to supply the value to the subtree.
called at module level; default value is used only when no Provider is present.
any value — primitive, object, or function. Changes trigger re-renders in all consumers.
a wrapper component owns useState and passes both value and setter in the context.
export useMyContext() that wraps useContext and throws if no Provider is found.
stabilise the value object to prevent consumers from re-rendering on unrelated parent renders.
closer Provider wins; enables scoped overrides within a subtree.
